text.fullText: From: mtaylor@megantaylor.org To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/18/2026 8:03:56 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ You don't often get email from mtaylor@megantaylor.org. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For five decades, this provision has served as a critical safeguard for the nation’s waterways by empowering states and authorized tribes to review federally permitted projects that may affect water quality within their jurisdictions. The proposed rule would sharply curtail this authority by limiting review to only “direct discharges” into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive evaluation of a project’s broader impacts on water resources. Such a narrow interpretation undermines the intent of Section 401 and would weaken the ability of states and tribes to protect drinking water sources, wetlands, fisheries, and culturally significant waters. In communities like Jackson Heights in Queens, the effects of weakened water protections would be tangible. Residents rely on safe, affordable drinking water supplied through regional watershed systems, and local parks and green spaces depend on healthy urban waterways and stormwater management infrastructure. Reduced oversight of federally permitted projects could increase the risk of contamination, strain aging infrastructure, and exacerbate flooding and water quality issues, particularly in dense, environmentally burdened neighborhoods. Limiting state review authority would diminish New York’s ability to safeguard the water resources that sustain local families, small businesses, and public health. Moreover, the proposed imposition of strict deadlines and a constrained review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring thorough environmental evaluation. This is especially troubling in light of recent judicial decisions that have already narrowed federal water protections. Further restricting state and tribal authority would compound these losses and leave communities more vulnerable. Water is a shared and irreplaceable resource that supports public health, ecological integrity, and economic vitality. Once degraded, restoration can take decades and impose substantial costs on communities. The existing Section 401 framework provides essential oversight that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful, science-based reviews to prevent long-term harm. I urge the EPA to withdraw the proposed rule and preserve the full authority of Section 401. Our nation’s waterways and the communities that depend on them deserve comprehensive protection grounded in sound environmental stewardship. Sincerely, New York
